USA.name: Donald Trump
USA.title: President of the United States
USA.tagline: Trying to contain a widening war with Iran while promising U.S. strength and fast results.
USA.domestic_detail: Supporters see decisive action; critics see strategic overreach and growing regional risk.
USA.global_detail: Allies depend on U.S. military power, but many also fear Washington is normalizing a larger regional war.
USA.bio: Trump is balancing direct military involvement, domestic political messaging, and pressure from partners who want both protection and a diplomatic exit. His core challenge is proving control over a conflict that is still widening.

USA.name: Marco Rubio
USA.title: Secretary of State
USA.tagline: Managing crisis diplomacy with Gulf states, Israel, and uneasy partners across the region.
USA.domestic_detail: He is viewed by many in Washington as a steadier public face of the administration's foreign policy.
USA.global_detail: Regional governments are looking to the State Department for reassurance, coordination, and off-ramp diplomacy.
USA.bio: Rubio's job is to keep allied governments aligned while preventing the war from producing a broader rupture in the Gulf, energy markets, and U.S. partnerships.

ISR.name: Benjamin Netanyahu
ISR.title: Prime Minister of Israel
ISR.tagline: Pushing simultaneous pressure on Iran and Hezbollah while defending the campaign as strategic necessity.
ISR.domestic_detail: He still commands a wartime constituency, but critics warn that the multi-front campaign is dangerously open-ended.
ISR.global_detail: Partners back Israel against Iran, but concern is rising over Lebanon operations and humanitarian fallout.
ISR.bio: Netanyahu is framing the current war as a chance to degrade Iran's regional network and reset Israel's security position after years of escalating proxy conflict.

ISR.name: Israel Katz
ISR.title: Defense Minister
ISR.tagline: Fronting the military pressure campaign against Hezbollah and Iran-linked targets.
ISR.domestic_detail: His posture appeals to security hardliners but worries Israelis concerned about a war with no clear end-state.
ISR.global_detail: Foreign diplomats see him as a clear sign that Israel is prioritizing coercion over caution.
ISR.bio: Katz is one of the strongest advocates of sustained military pressure in Lebanon and against Iranian infrastructure tied to regional attacks.

IRN.name: Mojtaba Khamenei
IRN.title: Supreme Leader of Iran
IRN.tagline: Elevated under fire and now responsible for wartime command as the Islamic Republic tries to project continuity.
IRN.domestic_detail: He inherits a system under direct military assault, with legitimacy still being tested inside the elite and the wider public.
IRN.global_detail: Outside governments expect him to be more rigid and more tightly tied to the Revolutionary Guard than his predecessors.
IRN.bio: Mojtaba Khamenei emerged as supreme leader during the most severe external shock the Islamic Republic has faced in decades. His immediate task is not reform but wartime survival and command consolidation.

IRN.name: Masoud Pezeshkian
IRN.title: President of Iran
IRN.tagline: Publicly defending the state under bombardment while trying to preserve a functioning civilian government.
IRN.domestic_detail: He is judged both by how he manages the war and by whether he can still offer any practical governance amid collapse pressures.
IRN.global_detail: Foreign governments see him as more pragmatic than the hardest security factions, but his room to maneuver is narrow.
IRN.bio: Pezeshkian has become the visible political face of a state trying to survive military blows, leadership transition, and international isolation all at once.

SAU.name: Mohammed bin Salman
SAU.title: Crown Prince and Prime Minister
SAU.tagline: Trying to keep Saudi Arabia secure, solvent, and out of direct war while the Gulf absorbs Iranian pressure.
SAU.domestic_detail: Many Saudis see him as the indispensable manager of security and economic continuity under crisis.
SAU.global_detail: Partners see MBS as central to Gulf coordination, though they also watch for signs of escalation.
SAU.bio: MBS wants to prove that Saudi Arabia can survive a regional war without becoming its main battleground. That means hard air defense, disciplined diplomacy, and relentless attention to oil infrastructure.

SAU.name: Faisal bin Farhan
SAU.title: Foreign Minister
SAU.tagline: Leading Saudi crisis diplomacy while keeping collective Gulf messaging coherent.
SAU.domestic_detail: He is seen as a credible steward of Saudi external messaging during a highly unstable period.
SAU.global_detail: His outreach matters because Gulf states need security guarantees and de-escalation at the same time.
SAU.bio: Faisal bin Farhan is helping Riyadh coordinate GCC positions, maintain Western support, and keep channels open even as the military environment worsens.

LBN.name: Joseph Aoun
LBN.title: President of Lebanon
LBN.tagline: Trying to preserve the state as war conditions overwhelm normal politics and expose Hezbollah-state tensions.
LBN.domestic_detail: He is one of the few national figures still seen as above day-to-day factional breakdown.
LBN.global_detail: Foreign governments view Aoun as a key partner for any post-war stabilization framework.
LBN.bio: Aoun is trying to keep Lebanon from becoming only a battlefield for other actors. His leverage is limited, but he remains central to any international effort to restore state authority.

LBN.name: Nawaf Salam
LBN.title: Prime Minister of Lebanon
LBN.tagline: Managing war administration, displacement pressure, and a shrinking space for reform politics.
LBN.domestic_detail: He still carries reformist credibility, but the war has pushed governance into pure crisis management.
LBN.global_detail: International donors and diplomats see Salam as one of the few Lebanese officials still trying to govern through institutions.
LBN.bio: Salam's government is juggling emergency relief, diplomacy, and the impossible task of defending state legitimacy while Hezbollah and Israel fight around it.

LBN.name: Naim Qassem
LBN.title: Secretary-General of Hezbollah
LBN.tagline: Keeping Hezbollah in the fight despite direct pressure from Israel and growing anger inside Lebanon.
LBN.domestic_detail: Supporters view Hezbollah as deterrence; critics see it as dragging Lebanon into catastrophe.
LBN.global_detail: Outside governments view him as a principal escalatory actor in the regional war.
LBN.bio: Qassem is trying to show Hezbollah still matters militarily and politically, even as the Lebanese state and the wider public pay a rising price.

YEM.name: Abdul-Malik al-Houthi
YEM.title: Houthi movement leader
YEM.tagline: Still able to threaten shipping and regional stability even without controlling all of Yemen.
YEM.domestic_detail: He commands a loyal base in Houthi-held areas but remains a central driver of war and repression.
YEM.global_detail: Regional powers and Western governments see him as a persistent maritime and missile threat.
YEM.bio: Al-Houthi retains leverage because a relatively small number of attacks can force a global shipping response and reshape regional risk perception.

YEM.name: Rashad al-Alimi
YEM.title: Head of Yemen's Presidential Leadership Council
YEM.tagline: Trying to keep the anti-Houthi camp together while the Red Sea and regional war distort Yemen's priorities.
YEM.domestic_detail: His authority depends heavily on coalition backing and the coherence of fragmented anti-Houthi factions.
YEM.global_detail: Partners see him as the formal face of Yemen's internationally recognized camp, but not as a decisive battlefield leader.
YEM.bio: Al-Alimi's challenge is political survival and coalition maintenance, not sweeping battlefield gains. Yemen's internal fractures still define his ceiling.

RUS.name: Vladimir Putin
RUS.title: President of Russia
RUS.tagline: Using sustained military pressure and limited talks to keep the initiative and test Western endurance.
RUS.domestic_detail: The state still controls the core political space, but the long war continues to consume resources and attention.
RUS.global_detail: Russia retains partners and commercial workarounds, but it remains strategically constrained by sanctions and war costs.
RUS.bio: Putin is still betting that time, attrition, and fractured Western focus will produce better political conditions than any compromise offered now.

RUS.name: Sergei Lavrov
RUS.title: Foreign Minister
RUS.tagline: Keeping diplomatic channels open just enough to reduce pressure without changing Russia's war aims.
RUS.domestic_detail: He remains one of the Kremlin's most durable foreign policy figures and is trusted by the system.
RUS.global_detail: Foreign governments treat him as an experienced negotiator, but not as a signal of softer Russian policy.
RUS.bio: Lavrov's task is to turn tactical diplomacy into strategic relief, whether through prisoner swaps, sanctions erosion, or blame shifting.

UKR.name: Volodymyr Zelenskyy
UKR.title: President of Ukraine
UKR.tagline: Trying to keep air defenses, aid, and diplomatic attention alive under relentless strike pressure.
UKR.domestic_detail: He still embodies resistance for many Ukrainians, though fatigue and loss remain severe.
UKR.global_detail: Partners still see Zelenskyy as the indispensable voice of Ukraine's defense and reconstruction case.
UKR.bio: Zelenskyy is balancing public morale, military necessity, and diplomacy in a year when other global crises are competing hard for attention.

UKR.name: Oleksandr Syrskyi
UKR.title: Commander-in-Chief of the Armed Forces
UKR.tagline: Managing a battlefield defined by air defense strain, drone adaptation, and constant Russian pressure.
UKR.domestic_detail: He is respected for stamina and control, but battlefield demands make every decision politically costly.
UKR.global_detail: Foreign militaries study Ukraine's command adaptations closely, especially on drones and dispersed defense.
UKR.bio: Syrskyi's job is not glamorous: hold the line, absorb strikes, innovate fast, and preserve enough combat power to keep Russia from converting pressure into collapse.

CHN.name: Xi Jinping
CHN.title: President of China
CHN.tagline: Pairing diplomatic calm with continued military modernization and an unchanged line on Taiwan.
CHN.domestic_detail: Xi still dominates the political system, though economic weakness makes performance legitimacy more important.
CHN.global_detail: Foreign governments treat Xi as both the key stabilizing interlocutor and the main driver of strategic rivalry.
CHN.bio: Xi is trying to stabilize ties with Washington enough to protect China's wider interests without softening on Taiwan, security, or industrial strategy.

CHN.name: Wang Yi
CHN.title: Foreign Minister
CHN.tagline: Selling a more stable tone abroad while preserving Beijing's hard red lines.
CHN.domestic_detail: He remains a trusted executor of Xi's external messaging.
CHN.global_detail: Counterparts see Wang as disciplined, experienced, and completely aligned with Beijing's hierarchy.
CHN.bio: Wang Yi is trying to create diplomatic space for summitry and trade stabilization without conceding anything substantive on Taiwan or China's military trajectory.

TWN.name: Lai Ching-te
TWN.title: President of Taiwan
TWN.tagline: Using a relative lull in Chinese air pressure to press for bigger, faster defense investments.
TWN.domestic_detail: Supporters see resolve; critics worry about cost, U.S. dependence, and confrontation with Beijing.
TWN.global_detail: Partners increasingly see Lai as serious about long-term defense reform, not just symbolic resistance.
TWN.bio: Lai is trying to convert broad awareness of the threat from China into durable defense capacity before Beijing decides to raise the pressure again.

TWN.name: Wellington Koo
TWN.title: Defense Minister
TWN.tagline: Overseeing the budget fight, arms acquisition pipeline, and the push for more resilient air defense.
TWN.domestic_detail: He sits at the center of Taiwan's debate over how much urgency is enough.
TWN.global_detail: Foreign defense officials treat Taiwan's procurement and readiness choices as a test of political seriousness.
TWN.bio: Koo is working the practical side of deterrence: procurement, stockpiles, interoperability, and making sure political messaging becomes actual capability.

PRK.name: Kim Jong Un
PRK.title: Leader of North Korea
PRK.tagline: Turning warship tests and missile theatrics into proof that North Korea's deterrent keeps expanding.
PRK.domestic_detail: The regime controls the narrative tightly and uses military spectacle to reinforce internal legitimacy.
PRK.global_detail: Outside governments see Kim as increasingly capable, more risk-tolerant, and still uninterested in real arms control.
PRK.bio: Kim is using naval modernization and strategic missile messaging to broaden North Korea's coercive toolkit beyond the familiar cycle of land-based tests.

SDN.name: Abdel Fattah al-Burhan
SDN.title: Army chief and de facto head of state
SDN.tagline: Trying to turn military gains into political legitimacy while Sudan's civilian catastrophe deepens.
SDN.domestic_detail: Some see him as the only counterweight to the RSF; many others see only another military ruler.
SDN.global_detail: Foreign governments work with Burhan because he controls formal state structures, not because confidence is high.
SDN.bio: Burhan remains essential to any state-level settlement in Sudan, but his military-first approach has not produced a path back to normal governance.

SDN.name: Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo
SDN.title: Leader of the Rapid Support Forces
SDN.tagline: Still central to the war despite mounting evidence of atrocity crimes and genocidal patterns.
SDN.domestic_detail: His power rests on armed force, coercion, and networked patronage rather than broad legitimacy.
SDN.global_detail: International scrutiny of RSF conduct has sharpened dramatically, especially after UN findings on El Fasher.
SDN.bio: Hemedti remains one of the most destabilizing figures in Sudan because he can still wage war even while his movement's crimes are drawing deeper international condemnation.

COD.name: Felix Tshisekedi
COD.title: President of the Democratic Republic of Congo
COD.tagline: Relying on outside pressure and diplomacy to blunt an insurgency the state still cannot fully reverse.
COD.domestic_detail: He is judged by whether he can restore state control in the east, which remains deeply uncertain.
COD.global_detail: Foreign partners still see Tshisekedi as the legitimate counterparty, but patience is thin.
COD.bio: Tshisekedi is trying to turn U.S. and regional pressure on Rwanda into better conditions on the ground, but eastern Congo remains only loosely governable.

COD.name: Corneille Nangaa
COD.title: AFC-M23 political figure
COD.tagline: Providing a political frame for an armed movement that still rules through territorial control.
COD.domestic_detail: Supporters present him as an alternative to Kinshasa; critics see him as the civilian face of rebellion.
COD.global_detail: Outside governments largely judge him through the violence and coercion linked to M23's expansion.
COD.bio: Nangaa matters because he tries to convert battlefield leverage into political bargaining power, which could shape how any eventual settlement is framed.

VEN.name: Delcy Rodriguez
VEN.title: Acting President of Venezuela
VEN.tagline: Trying to steer a controlled transition by mixing prisoner releases with diplomatic reopening.
VEN.domestic_detail: She still carries regime baggage, but some Venezuelans see the current phase as less closed than Maduro's rule.
VEN.global_detail: Foreign governments are testing whether Rodriguez is managing a real transition or only a tactical reset.
VEN.bio: Rodriguez is positioned between continuity and change. Her credibility depends on whether releases, monitoring, and election steps become institutional rather than symbolic.

VEN.name: Maria Corina Machado
VEN.title: Opposition leader
VEN.tagline: Still the most recognizable opposition figure, now trying to shape the next phase rather than only resist the old one.
VEN.domestic_detail: She remains a focal point for anti-regime energy, even if the political terrain has shifted.
VEN.global_detail: Foreign governments still treat Machado as a critical democratic benchmark for any Venezuelan transition.
VEN.bio: Machado's relevance has survived dramatic political changes because she still embodies the demand for a genuinely competitive post-Maduro order.

HTI.name: Alix Didier Fils-Aime
HTI.title: Prime Minister and interim leader of Haiti
HTI.tagline: Carrying the electoral timetable and security burden alone after the presidential council dissolved.
HTI.domestic_detail: Many Haitians want movement toward elections, but trust in interim authorities remains weak.
HTI.global_detail: He retains U.S. backing, which matters because Haiti's institutions still depend heavily on outside support.
HTI.bio: Fils-Aime has become the sole face of a transition still threatened by gangs, displacement, and fragile state capacity. His room for error is extremely small.

HTI.name: Andre Jonas Vladimir Paraison
HTI.title: Head of the Haitian National Police
HTI.tagline: Tasked with making an election security plan credible in a city where gangs still dominate most territory.
HTI.domestic_detail: Security institutions remain fragile, but police leadership is one of the few levers the state still has.
HTI.global_detail: International partners need the police to hold together because outside missions alone cannot stabilize Haiti.
HTI.bio: Paraison sits at the center of Haiti's immediate dilemma: elections cannot be credible without security, but security itself is still deeply contested.
